policy_statement: Create a Climate Equity Hub with partners to connect building owners/customers with service providers, installers, rebates/financing, and case studies (BO.3-2).
mechanism_description: Create a Climate Equity Hub with partners to connect building owners and customers to service providers and installers, rebates and financing, and case studies, which helps increase participation in building upgrades that reduce energy use and emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Program support/market facilitation → ↑ building retrofit & electrification uptake → ↓ fossil fuel combustion + ↓ energy demand → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: How building owners/tenants and contractors access retrofit services—bundling outreach, technical assistance, contractor connection, rebates, and financing through a centralized hub to increase participation. (2) Mediating process: Higher retrofit and electrification rates lead to improved building envelope/equipment efficiency and replacement of gas/oil appliances with electric alternatives, reducing delivered energy and direct combustion. (3) Outcome: Lower operational energy use and fuel-related emissions (and potentially lower peak demand depending on measures), resulting in net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased adoption of building upgrades that reduce on-site fossil fuel use and electricity demand, thereby reducing GHG emissions from building energy use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The hub is an enabling intervention whose primary effect is increased implementation of building decarbonization measures, which reduces energy use and/or shifts away from fossil fuels, cutting GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it improves affordability and access (equity/resource considerations), those are means to the main end—emissions reductions from building operations—so the dominant climate outcome is mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a hub focused mainly on resilience measures (e.g., backup power, cooling centers) it could shift toward adaptation, but this mechanism is explicitly tied to upgrades that “reduce energy use and emissions.”  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism (a decarbonization service hub to drive retrofit uptake) has its largest climate effect through increased adoption of emissions-reducing building upgrades, i.e., mitigation.  
5) Note on subtypes: Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., efficiency vs electrification emphasis; equity targeting; utility-led vs city-led) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some hubs may also deliver resource-efficiency co-benefits (reduced energy consumption) and equity outcomes; however, because the mechanism’s stated purpose is to drive emissions-reducing upgrades, mitigation remains primary.
key_indicators: "create a Climate Equity Hub"; "connect building owners and other customers"; "service providers and installers"; "rebates and financing"; "case studies"; "Partner with ... utilities ... CBO's"
co_benefits: Equity and affordability benefits (improved access to rebates/financing for affordable housing providers); local workforce/high-road job quality via "highroad service providers"; potential indoor air quality and health improvements from electrification.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as an incentive-program if the hub itself administers rebates/financing; however, the text emphasizes coordination/connection rather than providing funds directly, so classified as a planning/market-facilitation strategy.
